text: In the operating room, the surgeon attempts to manually reduce the stomach from the chest, but the hernia sac pulls it back up. The dissection begins anteriorly, developing the plane between the diaphragm and the hernia sac.

The surgeon dissects towards the right side, identifying the white line marking the edge of the right cruce, and establishes a plane connecting the dissection around the hiatus to the anterior. The dissection continues towards the left side of the hiatal opening, separating the thick hernia sac from the diaphragm. The surgeon approaches the left side of the hiatus and begins to take down the short gastric blood vessels, preparing for future fundoplication.

The surgeon approaches the left cruciate diaphragm, establishing a plane between the thick, chronic hernia sac and the diaphragm. Retraction of the hernia sac reveals posterior attachments from the gastric blood vessels. A Penrose drain is placed around the hernia sac for retraction, aiding in circumferential movement of the esophagus. Downward retraction of the esophagus is applied as the surgeon works into the mediastinum on the right side, separating easy attachments.

The surgeon lifts the esophagus and frees the hernia from the underlying spine and aorta, visible on the left and right of the screen, respectively. The dissection extends high into the chest, reaching the inferior pulmonary veins. A large mediastinal lipoma is resected from behind the gastroesophageal junction. Due to limited intra-abdominal esophagus length, a callous gastroplasty is performed using a blue load stapler, with the bougie guiding the stapling process. The stapler fires horizontally and obliquely towards the bougie, creating a wedge gastroplasty, and the excised fundus segment is prepared for pathology.

The surgeon creates a neoesophagus with appropriate intra-abdominal length. A Penrose drain is used for retraction, revealing a wide defect. The surgeon begins closing the hiatal defect using a running V-lock permanent suture, taking healthy bites of tissue with peritoneal lining. The suture is locked in place, and the esophagus is given some laxity to assess the remaining defect. A single Ethabon figure of eight suture is placed to support the lax anterior aspect of the diaphragm hiatus.

The surgeon performs a partial posterior Toupee fundoplication, delivering the neofundus around the posterior aspect and using a shoeshine maneuver to prevent twisting. Sutures are placed on the right aspect of the fundoplication, incorporating the top stitch into the diaphragm to secure the stomach and distal esophagus in the abdominal cavity. A gastropexy suture is placed to attach the posterior aspect of the fundoplication to the posterior cruce, aiming to prevent reherniation. The surgeon then works on the left side of the fundoplication, taking the greater curve aspect and completing the first and second stitches.

The surgeon completes the third stitch on the left side of the toupee fundoplication. The final product shows the stomach fully reduced with a toupee fundoplication and good intra-abdominal length achieved through the collis gastroplasty.
